cot_prefix: Okay, let me try to figure this out. So, there are five fruits: apples, oranges, watermelons, pears, and mangoes. They are arranged in order of price, from most to least expensive. The problem gives me a few clues about their relative prices, and I need to determine which one is the second-most expensive. First, let me list out the given information: 1. Apples are more expensive than oranges. 2. Oranges are more expensive than mangoes. 3. Apples are the third-most expensive. 4. Pears are more expensive than watermelons. From this, I need to deduce the order of the fruits from most to least expensive. Starting with the first three clues. Let me consider the relative positions of apples, oranges, and mangoes. We know that apples > oranges > mangoes in terms of price. Also, apples are the third-most expensive. That means there are two fruits more expensive than apples. So, if we consider the order from most to least expensive, apples are in position 3 (third place). Then, there must be two fruits more expensive
